summary: The post explores the concept of the 'entrepreneurial gap'—giving people more accountability than they have direct resources to accomplish. This approach works brilliantly in startups and growth companies when teams share aligned goals, encouraging cross-functional collaboration and creative problem-solving. However, the same dynamic becomes toxic when organizations have misaligned or competing goals, leading to political maneuvering rather than collaboration. The key insight is that organizational alignment is the prerequisite that makes stretching people beyond their direct control productive rather than destructive.
key_points: The entrepreneurial gap means holding people accountable for outcomes bigger than their direct resources can accomplish
This approach thrives in startups because entrepreneurial employees find ways to gather support and align efforts across the organization
Successful implementations require company-wide alignment on a few clear goals, often centered on the customer
Without alignment, the entrepreneurial gap creates toxic politics as peers compete for scarce resources toward different outcomes
The balance requires both stretching people beyond their control AND ensuring organizational alignment
money_quotes: The idea is straightforward: give people more accountability than they have the direct resources to accomplish.
In many organizations employees are generally held accountable only for things fully within their control.

Because early startup employees tend to be very entrepreneurial they often find a way to do just that, to gather support from other people in the organization and align efforts to produce outsized results.

In companies where everyone is working ultimately towards the same goals, the entrepreneurial gap is awesome because it encourages people to work together and identify opportunities and efficiencies, especially cross-functionally, that may otherwise be lost.

When you are in direct competition with your peers for scarce resources and you are not going to be graded on the same outcomes, the entrepreneurial gap produces a toxic environment of politics and power plays.
Organizational alignment is important because it lets you successfully ask more from people than the resources they have at hand.
Without organizational alignment, you get political maneuvering. Without stretching people beyond their direct control, you get a lack of collaboration and creative cross-functional engagement.
key_insight: Stretching people beyond their direct resources only produces collaboration and innovation when the organization is aligned on shared goals; without that alignment, the same dynamic breeds destructive politics.
